---
title: BIOS 731 HW4
author: Liam Hanson
format: html
execute: 
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
resources:
- ../output/
---


## Problem 1: Gibbs Sampler

We derive the Gibbs Sampler updates for class labels $c_i$ and the mean of the $k$th Gaussian $\mu_k$. The full conditional for $C_{ik}$ i.e. the probability observation $i$ is predicted to have mean $\mu_k$ is

$$P(C_{ik} = 1 | y_i, \mu_k) = \frac{P(y_i, \mu_k | C_{ik})P(C_{ik}=1)}{P(y_i, \mu_k)} \\
\propto P(y_i, mu_k | C_{ik})P(\mu_k)P(C_{ik}=1)$$

due to independence of $\mu$ and $C_i$. Then $P(C_{ik}=1)=\frac{1}{K}$, and using the Gaussian likelihood $p(y_i|\mu_k, C_{ik}=1)$ and Gaussian prior $p(\mu_k)$ we obtain that the full conditional for $C_i$ is proportional to

$$\textrm{exp}(-\frac{1}{2}(y_i-\mu_k)^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mu_k^2)$$.

To obtain valid probabilities with which to sample, for every $y_i$ we compute this expression for each $\mu_k$ and normalize the resulting $k$-length vector by its sum. The sampled label is the update for $C_ik$ for this iteration.

To obtain the full conditional distribution for $\mu_k$, follow similar steps as above to obtain

$$p(\mu_k | y_i, C_{ik} = 1) \propto \textrm{exp}(-\frac{1}{2}(y_i-\mu_k)^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mu_k^2)$$.

Because the likelihood and the prior of $\mu_k$ are Gaussian, they yield a Gaussian posterior which we obtain by completing the square:

$$\textrm{exp}(-\frac{1}{2}(y_i-\mu_k)^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mu_k^2) $$
$$=\textrm{exp}(-\frac{1}{2}((1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2})\mu_k^2-2y_i\mu_k+y_i^2)))$$  $$=\textrm{exp}(-\frac{1}{2}(1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2})(\mu_k^2-\frac{2y_i\mu_k}{1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2}}+(\frac{y_i}{1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2}})^2-(\frac{y_i}{1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2}})^2+y_i^2)))$$ 
$$\propto \textrm{exp}(-\frac{1}{2}(1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2})(\mu_k - \frac{y_i}{1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2}})^2)$$

which implies $\mu_k | y_i, C_{ik} \sim \textrm{N}(\frac{y_i}{1+\frac{1}{\sigma^2}}, \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2+1})$.

The update for $mu_k$ is then sampled from this distribution.

## Problem 2: Variational Inference

We now derive the updates for variational parameters $m_k, s^2_k, phi_k$. The ELBO is

$$ELBO(q) = \textrm{E[log} \space p(z)] + \textrm{E[log} \space p(x|z)] - \textrm{E[log} \space q(z)]$$.

By the Mean Field Assumption, we have

$$q(z) = \prod_{k=1}^K q_k(\mu_k; m_k, s^2_k)\prod_{i=1}^n q(c_i ; \phi_i)$$

First we derive the update for $\bf{\phi}_i$.

$$q_i(c_i ; \phi_i)^* \propto \textrm{exp}\{\textrm{E}_{-\phi_i}[\textrm{log}\space p(\mu, c_i, c_{-i}, y)]\} \\ 
$$
$$ = \textrm{exp}\{-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{k=1}^KC_{ik}\textrm{E}_{-\phi_i}[(x_k-\mu_k)^2]\}$$
$$\propto \textrm{exp}\{x_im_k-\frac{m_k^2+s_k^2}{2}\}$$
so the update for $\phi_{ik}$ is 
$$\phi_{ik}=\textrm{exp}\{x_im_k-\frac{m_k^2+s_k^2}{2}\}$$
For $mu_k$,
$$q_k(\mu_k ; m_k, s^2_k)^* \propto \textrm{exp}\{\textrm{E}_{-\mu_k}[\textrm{log}\space p(\mu_k, \mu_{-k}, c, y)]\} \\ 
$$

$$\propto \textrm{exp}\{(\sum_{i=1}^n{x_i\phi_{ik}})\mu_k- (\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n\phi_{ik}+\frac{1}{2\sigma^2})\mu_k^2\}$$
which is proportional to a Gaussian with sufficient statistics $\sum\mu_k$ and 
$\sum\mu_k^2$ and natural parameters $\sum_{i=1}^n{x_i}\phi_{ik}$,$(\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n\phi_{ik}+\frac{1}{2\sigma^2})$.
Solving for the mean $m_k$ and variance $s_k^2$ of this Gaussian gives updates
$$s^2_k = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^n\phi_{ik} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2}}$$ and 
$$m_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n{x_i}\phi_{ik}}{\sum_{i=1}^n\phi_{ik} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2}}$$.


## Problem 3: Fit to Faithful data set

To fit the Old Faithful waiting time data set as a Gaussian 2-mixture, we use the derived CAVI algorithm and Gibbs Sampler with 3 chains of 10000 samples with 2000 burn-in samples. The estimates for $\mu_1$, $\mu_2$, and computation time are given below.


Method|$\mu_1$ | $\mu_2$ | Mean computation time (s)
-----|-----|-----|-----:
CAVI|54.74|80.28| 0.17
Gibbs Sampler|54.87 | 80.35 | 4.23

The confusion matrix between the two algorithms estimated labels was

|  | Gibbs  ($c_i=1$)   | Gibbs  ($c_i=2$)    | Total |
| ----- | -------- | ----------- | ----- |
| **CAVI**  ($c_i=1$)| 100  | 0      | 100 |
| **CAVI**   ($c_i=2$)  | 1 | 171 | 172 |
| Total | 101 | 171 | 272 |

We can see nearly perfect agreement between the methods.

Based on trace plots, lag plots and effective sample size, the Gibbs sampler mixed well and converged quickly.

All effective sample sizes for each $\mu_k$ and each chain were 10000 except for the 2nd chain for $\mu_2$.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| layout-nrow: 2
#| fig.cap: "10-sample average shown. Autocorrelation was low and sampler generally mixed and converged quickly."
knitr::include_graphics(c("output/mu_1_trace_plot.png",
                          "output/mu_2_trace_plot.png")
)
```
Lag plots for $\mu_2$ chain 2 showed relatively high autocorrelation compared to 
other chains.


```{r}
#| echo: false
#| layout-nrow: 2
#| out.width: ["40%"]
knitr::include_graphics(c("output/mu_1_lag_plot.png",
                          "output/mu_2_lag_plot.png")
)
```

## Problem 4: Simulation Study

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| layout-nrow: 2
#| out.width: ["40%"]
knitr::include_graphics(c("output/bias_plot.png",
                          "output/cov_plot.png")
)
```
In general, the gibbs sampler estimates were more biased then the variational inference estimates. Bias was highest for $k=3$ i.e. $\mu_3=10$. Biased increased from $\mu_1$
to $\mu_3$ but then sharpl decreased for $mu_4=20$. This is likely because observations
generated from this gaussian were better separated from the rest of the sample
and quicker to label and thus converge on the true mean. 

Coverage of the true mean in the Gibbs sampler was somewhat unpredictable. Simulations
with n = 1000 appeared to be closes to nominal coverage as well as the better separated
$\mu_4$. It may be that for very high sample sizes, the Gibbs sampler posterior means
converged very tightly to the sample mean of their data and credible intervals 
were too narrow to include the true mean.
